Q1.bottleneck: Already excellent performance (42ms avg), 2x faster than DuckDB. Minor optimization opportunity in aggregation phase.
Q1.root_cause: Uses hash aggregation for only 4 groups when sorted aggregation would be faster. Hash table overhead dominates for ultra-low cardinality GROUP BY.

Q3.bottleneck: PRIMARY BOTTLENECK - 3.4x slower than DuckDB (339ms vs 99ms). Hash table operations dominate execution time.
Q3.root_cause: std::unordered_map is cache-unfriendly and has high overhead (bucket chaining, poor cache locality). Building 1.46M-entry hash table for filtered_orders and probing it 36M times. NUMA-unaware memory access across 2 sockets. String comparison for c_mktsegment (10-byte strings). No SIMD for arithmetic or filtering.

Q6.bottleneck: Already excellent performance (10ms avg), 2x faster than DuckDB. Minor optimization opportunity in filter evaluation.
Q6.root_cause: Simple parallel scan-filter-aggregate is well-optimized. Could use SIMD for filter evaluation (4-predicate AND chain) and arithmetic.

analysis.overall: Iteration 1 was highly successful: Q1 and Q6 are already 2x faster than DuckDB. Q3 is the clear bottleneck (3.4x slower than DuckDB). Root cause: std::unordered_map has 5-10ns overhead per lookup (cache misses, bucket chaining), and Q3 does 36M+ hash probes. Replacing with Robin Hood hashing (open addressing, better cache locality) should yield 2-3x speedup on Q3. Secondary issues: NUMA-unaware memory access (cross-socket latency), no SIMD utilization, string comparisons. Hardware has 64 cores with 2 NUMA nodes - current parallelism strategy doesn't account for NUMA topology.

performance_optimizations.description: Replace std::unordered_map with Robin Hood hash tables (open addressing) in Q3's hash join. std::unordered_map uses bucket chaining (linked lists), causing cache misses on every probe. Robin Hood hashing uses open addressing with backward shift deletion, achieving 2-5x faster lookups. Implement custom RobinHoodMap<K,V> or use abseil/folly flat_hash_map. Apply to: (1) filtered_customers hash set (~300K entries), (2) filtered_orders hash table (~1.46M entries), (3) aggregation hash table (~400K groups). Expected improvement: 2-3x speedup on Q3 hash join operations, reducing Q3 from 339ms to ~150-200ms. DuckDB uses similar techniques (flat hash tables with SIMD probing). Files to modify: queries/q3.cpp (replace std::unordered_map/set with RobinHoodMap/Set), create new data-structures/robin_hood_map.h (or add abseil dependency).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-3x speedup on Q3 (339ms → 150-200ms). Robin Hood hashing has ~3-5ns lookup vs std::unordered_map's 8-12ns. Q3 does 36M+ hash probes (lineitem scan probing orders hash table), so 5ns savings per probe = 180ms total savings. This brings Q3 within striking distance of DuckDB (99ms).

performance_optimizations.description: c_mktsegment filter in Q3 currently compares 10-byte strings ("BUILDING") 1.5M times. Since c_mktsegment has only 5 unique values, dictionary-encode it during ingestion (map strings to int8_t codes 0-4). Modify ingestion to build dictionary and write encoded column. Modify Q3 to compare encoded integers (1 byte) instead of strings (10 bytes). Expected improvement: 5-10x faster string filtering. Files to modify: ingest.cpp (add dictionary encoding for c_mktsegment), storage/storage.cpp (add load_dictionary/encode_string functions), queries/q3.cpp (use encoded comparison: encoded_mktsegment[i] == BUILDING_CODE instead of c_mktsegment[i] == "BUILDING").
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-20ms reduction on Q3. String comparison is ~10-20ns for 10-byte strings (memory load + strncmp), vs 1-2ns for int8_t comparison. 1.5M comparisons × 10ns savings = 15ms. Small but worthwhile for low-risk change.

performance_optimizations.description: Q3 computes revenue = l_extendedprice * (1.0 - l_discount) for 36M rows passing filters. Vectorize this using AVX2 (process 4 doubles per instruction). Use _mm256_load_pd to load 4 extendedprices, _mm256_load_pd for 4 discounts, _mm256_set1_pd(1.0) for constant, _mm256_sub_pd for (1-discount), _mm256_mul_pd for final product. Process lineitem scan in chunks of 4 rows. Expected improvement: 3-4x faster arithmetic (1 SIMD instruction vs 4 scalar ops). Files to modify: queries/q3.cpp (add SIMD-vectorized revenue computation in lineitem scan loop, use #ifdef __AVX2__ guard, provide scalar fallback).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-20ms reduction on Q3. Arithmetic is ~5-10% of Q3 runtime (36M multiplications). 4x speedup on arithmetic = 10-20ms savings. Combined with Robin Hood hashing, Q3 should reach ~130-180ms.

performance_optimizations.technique: Sorted Aggregation for Low-Cardinality GROUP BY
performance_optimizations.description: Q1 uses hash aggregation for 4 groups (l_returnflag × l_linestatus). Hash table overhead (hashing, probing, bucket management) is overkill. Switch to sorted aggregation: (1) create array of 4 group states indexed by composite key (returnflag=0-2, linestatus=0-1 → index = returnflag*2 + linestatus), (2) directly index into array instead of hash lookup, (3) eliminate hash table merge overhead (merge 4-element arrays per thread is trivial). Expected improvement: 2-3x faster aggregation, reducing Q1 from 42ms to ~20-30ms. Files to modify: queries/q1.cpp (replace ParallelHashAgg with direct array indexing: thread_local std::array<Q1AggResult, 4> local_agg, index = (returnflag - 'A') * 2 + (linestatus == 'O' ? 1 : 0)).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-30ms reduction on Q1 (42ms → 15-25ms). Hash aggregation overhead is ~50% of Q1 runtime (hash computation, probing, bucket management for 60M rows). Direct array indexing is 1-2ns vs hash lookup's 5-8ns. 60M rows × 5ns savings = 300ms savings... but merge overhead is negligible for 4 groups, so practical savings ~20-30ms. Brings Q1 to ~15-25ms (5-10x faster than DuckDB!).

performance_optimizations.description: Q6 evaluates 4 predicates per row (60M rows): date range, discount range, quantity threshold. Vectorize using AVX2 to process 4 rows at a time. Load 4 shipdates, compare with _mm256_cmpge_epi32 (shipdate >= start) and _mm256_cmplt_epi32 (shipdate < end), AND with _mm256_and_si256. Repeat for discount (convert to int32 or use double comparison) and quantity. Use _mm256_movemask_epi8 to extract passing rows. Expected improvement: 3-4x faster filter evaluation. Files to modify: queries/q6.cpp (add SIMD filter loop, use #ifdef __AVX2__ guard, provide scalar fallback).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10ms reduction on Q6 (10ms → 5-8ms). Filter evaluation is ~40% of Q6 runtime (4 comparisons × 60M rows). 4x speedup on filters = 5-10ms savings. Brings Q6 to ~5-8ms (3-4x faster than DuckDB!).

performance_optimizations.description: System has 2 NUMA nodes (32 cores each). Current code allocates hash tables on one node, then all 64 threads access them, causing cross-socket memory traffic (120ns latency vs 60ns local). Use NUMA-aware allocation: (1) detect NUMA topology with numa_available(), (2) partition data across NUMA nodes (first 32 threads process rows on node 0, next 32 on node 1), (3) allocate thread-local hash tables with numa_alloc_onnode to ensure local allocation. For Q3's filtered_orders hash table (accessed by all threads), replicate it on both NUMA nodes. Expected improvement: 1.5-2x speedup on cross-socket accesses. Files to modify: queries/q3.cpp (add NUMA-aware partitioning in parallel loops, replicate filtered_orders hash table per node), CMakeLists.txt (link -lnuma).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-40ms reduction on Q3. Cross-socket memory access is ~50% of Q3's memory traffic (lineitem scan on one node probing orders hash table on another). Eliminating cross-socket latency (120ns → 60ns) for 36M probes = 36M × 60ns = 2.2 seconds... but cache effects reduce this to ~20-40ms practical savings. Combined with other optimizations, Q3 should reach ~100-130ms (competitive with DuckDB).

storage_changes.change: Dictionary-encode c_mktsegment column (5 unique values → int8_t codes 0-4). Store dictionary as separate metadata file (mktsegment_dict.bin) and encoded column as int8_t array.
storage_changes.rationale: Reduces c_mktsegment storage from 10 bytes/row to 1 byte/row (10x compression). Enables integer comparison (1-2ns) instead of string comparison (10-20ns) in Q3 filter. 1.5M customer rows × 9 bytes saved = 13.5MB savings (negligible), but 5-10x faster filtering is significant. String dictionary encoding is standard in modern OLAP (DuckDB, ClickHouse use it heavily).

summary: Iteration 1 achieved excellent results: Q1 and Q6 are already 2x faster than DuckDB. Q3 is the clear bottleneck at 3.4x slower than DuckDB (339ms vs 99ms). Primary recommendation: Replace std::unordered_map with Robin Hood hash tables in Q3 (Priority 1, expected 2-3x speedup). Secondary optimizations: dictionary-encode c_mktsegment strings (Priority 2), add SIMD arithmetic for revenue computation (Priority 3), NUMA-aware memory allocation (Priority 6). For Q1: switch to sorted aggregation for ultra-low cardinality GROUP BY (Priority 4, expected 2x speedup). For Q6: add SIMD filter evaluation (Priority 5). Expected final performance after all optimizations: Q1 ~15-25ms (5-10x faster than DuckDB), Q3 ~100-150ms (competitive with DuckDB), Q6 ~5-8ms (3-4x faster than DuckDB). Total execution time: ~120-180ms vs current 450ms (2.5-3.7x speedup). Focus on Priority 1-3 for maximum impact with lowest risk.
